did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses AI methods including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), MobileNet, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Support Vector Machines (SVM), and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) to classify skin images for dermatological conditions such as Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma, as well as nail abnormalities like Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Beaus Line, and Koilonychia, to predict vitamin deficiencies.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a 'Nutri Scan AI System' using image processing and neural networks (CNN, MobileNet, LSTM, SVM). It includes a proposed system architecture, training of models on datasets, performance evaluation with specific metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score), and results analysis, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Nutri Scan AI System Using Image Processing and Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes the Nutri Scan AI System, a non-invasive tool that uses image processing and neural networks to detect vitamin deficiencies by analyzing visual symptoms in nail, tongue, and skin images. The system employs machine learning models including Support Vector Machines (SVM), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and a hybrid MobileNet-LSTM architecture to classify abnormalities such as Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Koilonychia, and various skin carcinomas. A Flask-based web application was developed to allow users to upload images and receive personalized vitamin recommendations and dietary guidance. The models were trained on datasets of nail, tongue, and skin images, with performance evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. Results indicated that the MobileNet model achieved the highest accuracy of 96.1% for nail/tongue classification and 90.50% for skin disease classification, outperforming CNN, SVM, and ANN models. The study concludes that this AI-driven approach offers a cost-effective and efficient method for early vitamin deficiency detection compared to traditional invasive blood tests.

all_task_types.target: Vitamin deficiency categories and specific dermatological/nail/tongue abnormalities (e.g., Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Koilonychia, Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task. Model 1 classifies nail and tongue images into 11 categories of vitamin deficiencies/abnormalities. Model 2 classifies skin images into 11 classes (including Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, etc.) to predict associated vitamin deficiencies. The system outputs discrete diagnostic categories rather than continuous values. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Nail and Tongue Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of nail and tongue images used for Model 1 to classify vitamin deficiencies and abnormalities. The specific source (public/private) is not explicitly named, but it is described as 'diverse datasets' obtained for the study.

use.training: Used to train Model 1 (SVM, CNN, MobileNet, LSTM) for classifying nail and tongue abnormalities.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.

datasets_used.name: Skin Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of skin images used for Model 2 to classify skin diseases linked to vitamin deficiencies. The specific source (public/private) is not explicitly named, but it is described as 'diverse datasets' obtained for the study.

use.training: Used to train Model 2 (CNN, MobileNet-LSTM) for classifying skin diseases.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance, identify strengths in correct classification, and highlight challenges in distinguishing between similar lesion types.
To tune hyperparameters (regularization parameter C, kernel coefficient gamma, and kernel type) for the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier.
To quantitatively compare the performance of different machine learning models (SVM, CNN, MobileNet, LSTM, ANN) on nail/tongue and skin image classification tasks.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics, and comparisons were made based on these descriptive statistics without inferential statistical testing.

Model 1 (Nail and Tongue Image Classific.dataset_description: Diverse datasets of nail and tongue images labeled for 11 vitamin deficiency categories (e.g., Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Beau's Line, Koilonychia, Pitting, Clubbing, Blue Finger, Lindsay’s Nail, Muehrcke's Lines, Onychogryphosis, Terry’s Nail, Pernicious Anemia).

MobileNet.notes: Achieved high accuracy. The paper states 'results from both the MobileNet models show high accuracy' and specifically cites 96.1% for MobileNet in this section.

CNN.notes: Specific metric value not reported for Model 1, only that MobileNet achieved 96.1%.

SVM.notes: Specific metric value not reported for Model 1.

LSTM.notes: Specific metric value not reported for Model 1.

Model 1 (Nail and Tongue Image Classific.notes: The results indicate that both MobileNet models performed exceptionally well in classifying nail images into the 11 categories of vitamin deficiencies. No specific precision, recall, or F1 scores are provided for Model 1 in the text.

Model 2 (Skin Image Classification).dataset_description: Diverse datasets of skin images labeled for 11 classes including Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Dermatofibroma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus, and Vascular Lesion.

CNN.notes: The CNN model overfitted, with 90% training accuracy but only 20% validation accuracy.

Hybrid Model (LSTM + MobileNet).notes: Achieved moderate performance. The text does not provide a single aggregate accuracy percentage for the hybrid model in Table I, but describes its training/validation accuracy as 50%/40%.

Model 2 (Skin Image Classification).notes: MobileNet outperformed other models for skin disease classification with 90.50% accuracy. The Hybrid Model (LSTM + MobileNet) achieved moderate performance with 50% training and 40% validation accuracy. The CNN model showed signs of overfitting.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple architectures were used: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), MobileNet (specifically MobileNetV2), and a Hybrid MobileNet-LSTM model.
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV2 base model was used with pre-trained ImageNet weights (include_top=False).
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (for CNN and MobileNet); 299x299 pixels (for MobileNet); 1D arrays (for SVM and ANN).

CNN.layers: Convolutional layers with ReLU activation, followed by Max Pooling layers for dimension reduction, and Fully Connected layers.

ANN.layers: Input layer, hidden layers with 128, 64, and 32 units, output layer.

Hybrid_MobileNet_LSTM.components: MobileNet for feature extraction (convolutional layers unchanged, classification section stripped) followed by LSTM layers for sequential pattern detection.
Hybrid_MobileNet_LSTM.input_processing: Feature maps converted to sequence formats for LSTM.

SVM.tuning: GridSearchCV for C, gamma, and kernel type.

training_details.epochs: 30 epochs (CNN, ANN); 30-40 cycles (MobileNet/Hybrid); unspecified for SVM.
training_details.regularization: Dropout layer used in MobileNet/Hybrid model to prevent overfitting.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to standard dimensions (224x224 pixels for CNN and MobileNet; 299x299 pixels for MobileNet; 150x150 or 224x224 pixels for CNN grayscale input)
Normalization of pixel values to achieve consistent data values
Conversion of images to 1D arrays for SVM and ANN models
Conversion of images to grayscale dimensions for CNN input
Data augmentation via ImageDataGenerator including rescaling, flipping, rotation, zooming, and resizing
Use of pre-trained ImageNet weights with MobileNetV2 (include_top=False)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied resizing, image flipping, and zooming to add diversity to the dataset for the Convolutional Neural Network model.
Applied rescaling and flipping methods to enhance dataset diversity for the MobileNet model.
Used rotation, flipping, and zooming actions to develop stronger model performance and reduce overfitting effects.

conclusions: The MobileNet model achieved the highest accuracy of 96.1% for nail and tongue image classification and 90.50% for skin disease classification, outperforming CNN, SVM, and ANN models.
The CNN model exhibited significant overfitting in skin classification, with 90% training accuracy but only 20% validation accuracy, indicating a need for regularization.
The Hybrid MobileNet-LSTM model demonstrated moderate performance with 50% training and 40% validation accuracy, suggesting room for improvement through hyperparameter tuning or data augmentation.
MobileNet proved to be the most effective model for skin disease classification, balancing computational efficiency with high accuracy, making it suitable for lightweight and real-time deployments.
The proposed Nutri Scan AI System offers a non-invasive, cost-effective, and accessible alternative to traditional invasive blood tests for early vitamin deficiency detection.
The system successfully integrates machine learning and deep learning techniques to analyze visual symptoms in nails, tongue, and skin to predict deficiencies in vitamins such as B12, A, Iron, and C.
The implementation of a Flask-based web application allows for user-friendly image upload and provides personalized vitamin recommendations and dietary guidance.
important_remarks: The paper lacks transparency regarding dataset provenance; it describes the data only as 'diverse datasets' without specifying sources, sizes, or class distributions, raising concerns about data leakage risks and reproducibility.

The CNN model for skin classification exhibited severe overfitting, achieving 90% training accuracy but only 20% validation accuracy, indicating poor generalization despite the reported 90% test accuracy in the summary (likely a reporting inconsistency or different split).
The Hybrid MobileNet-LSTM model showed moderate performance with 50% training and 40% validation accuracy, suggesting the complex architecture did not outperform the simpler MobileNet model.

The study links specific dermatological conditions (e.g., Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma) directly to vitamin deficiencies (A, D, C) without providing clinical evidence or citations for these specific associations, which may mislead clinical relevance.

There is a notable inconsistency in the text where 'Melanoma' is listed under tongue disorders in Section I, which is anatomically incorrect and suggests potential data labeling errors or textual confusion.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals), relying solely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) which limits the strength of the comparative evidence.

The system claims to detect 'Pernicious Anemia' via tongue images, a condition typically diagnosed via blood tests for B12 and intrinsic factor antibodies, raising questions about the validity of using visual tongue features as a proxy for this specific systemic condition.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (INCET 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous peer review compared to high-impact medical journals.
